title: How to Quantify PCR Products Using Real-Time qPCR and Relative Quantification    Relative Quantification in Real-Time PCR: Worked Example with ΔΔCt and Pfaffl Methods
author: Hussain, Zahid
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
   How to Quantify PCR Products Using Real-Time qPCR and Relative Quantification (with (ΔΔCt) Method with Example Calculation).  
   
   
 worked example of  relative quantification (ΔΔCt method)  for real-time qPCR, plus the  Pfaffl correction  if PCR efficiencies differ. I show every arithmetic step so you can follow exactly. 
   
  Experimental setup (example)  
 You measure a  target gene  and a  housekeeping reference (GAPDH)  in  Control  and  Treated  samples, in triplicate. 
 Raw Ct values: 
 
  Control  
 
 Target Ct (triplicates): 22.5, 22.7, 22.6 
 Reference Ct (triplicates): 18.0, 17.9, 18.1 
 
  Treated  
 
 Target Ct (triplicates): 19.8, 19.9, 20.0 
 Reference Ct (triplicates): 17.5, 17.6, 17.4 
 
 
 Assume PCR efficiency ≈  100% (E = 2.0)  for the ΔΔCt method. Later we’ll apply Pfaffl with non-ideal efficiencies:  E_target = 1.95 ,  E_ref = 2.00 . 
   
  Step 1 — compute average Ct for each group  
 Control target average: 
 
 (22.5 + 22.7 + 22.6) / 3 
 Sum = 67.8 → 67.8 / 3 =  22.6  
 
 Control reference average: 
 
 (18.0 + 17.9 + 18.1) / 3 
 Sum = 54.0 → 54.0 / 3 =  18.0  
 
 Treated target average: 
 
 (19.8 + 19.9 + 20.0) / 3 
 Sum = 59.7 → 59.7 / 3 =  19.9  
 
 Treated reference average: 
 
 (17.5 + 17.6 + 17.4) / 3 
 Sum = 52.5 → 52.5 / 3 =  17.5  
 
   
  Step 2 — compute ΔCt (target normalized to reference)  
 ΔCt = Ct_target − Ct_reference 
 
  ΔCt_control  = 22.6 − 18.0 =  4.6  
  ΔCt_treated  = 19.9 − 17.5 =  2.4  
 
   
  Step 3 — compute ΔΔCt  
 ΔΔCt = ΔCt_treated − ΔCt_control 
 
 ΔΔCt = 2.4 − 4.6 =  −2.2  
 
 A negative ΔΔCt means the target is more abundant in Treated (lower Ct after normalization). 
   
  Step 4 — fold change by ΔΔCt method (assumes E = 2)  
 Fold change (Treated / Control) = 2−ΔΔCt2^{-\Delta\Delta Ct}2−ΔΔCt 
 
 −ΔΔCt=−(−2.2)=2.2-\Delta\Delta Ct = -(-2.2) = 2.2−ΔΔCt=−(−2.2)=2.2 
 Fold change = 22.22^{2.2}22.2 
 
 Compute 22.22^{2.2}22.2: 
 
 22.2=22×20.2=4×20.22^{2.2} = 2^{2} \times 2^{0.2} = 4 \times 2^{0.2}22.2=22×20.2=4×20.2 
 20.2=e0.2ln⁡2≈e0.2×0.693147=e0.1386294≈1.14872^{0.2} = e^{0.2 \ln 2} \approx e^{0.2 \times 0.693147} = e^{0.1386294} \approx 1.148720.2=e0.2ln2≈e0.2×0.693147=e0.1386294≈1.1487 
 So 22.2≈4×1.1487=4.59482^{2.2} \approx 4 \times 1.1487 = 4.594822.2≈4×1.1487=4.5948 
 
  Result (ΔΔCt method): Treated is ≈ 4.59-fold upregulated vs Control.  
   
  If PCR efficiencies are not exactly 2 — use the Pfaffl method  
 Pfaffl ratio formula: 
 Ratio=Etarget(ΔCttarget,  control−treated)Eref(ΔCtref,  control−treated)\text{Ratio} = \frac{E_{target}^{(\Delta Ct_{target,\;control-treated})}}{E_{ref}^{(\Delta Ct_{ref,\;control-treated})}}Ratio=Eref(ΔCtref,control−treated)Etarget(ΔCttarget,control−treated) 
 where ΔCttarget,  control−treated=Cttarget,control−Cttarget,treated\Delta Ct_{target,\;control-treated} = Ct_{target,control} - Ct_{target,treated}ΔCttarget,control−treated=Cttarget,control−Cttarget,treated, and similarly for reference. 
 From our averages: 
 
 ΔCttarget,  control−treated=22.6−19.9=2.7\Delta Ct_{target,\;control-treated} = 22.6 - 19.9 = 2.7ΔCttarget,control−treated=22.6−19.9=2.7 
 ΔCtref,  control−treated=18.0−17.5=0.5\Delta Ct_{ref,\;control-treated} = 18.0 - 17.5 = 0.5ΔCtref,control−treated=18.0−17.5=0.5 
 
 With Etarget=1.95E_{target}=1.95Etarget=1.95, Eref=2.00E_{ref}=2.00Eref=2.00: 
 Ratio=1.952.72.000.5\text{Ratio} = \frac{1.95^{2.7}}{2.00^{0.5}}Ratio=2.000.51.952.7 
 Compute numerically: 
 
 1.952.7≈1.952.7≈1.95^{2.7} \approx 1.95^{2.7} \approx 1.952.7≈1.952.7≈ (calculation) ≈  2.9887?  — (we’ll show final accurate value) 
 2.000.5=2≈1.414213562.00^{0.5} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414213562.000.5=2≈1.41421356 
 
 Using the precise evaluation: 
 
 1.952.7≈6.07381.95^{2.7} \approx 6.07381.952.7≈6.0738 — hold on, we should calculate correctly. 
 
 (Accurate calculation done programmatically): 
 
 1.952.7≈6.06851.95^{2.7} \approx 6.06851.952.7≈6.0685 — (final below) 
 Dividing by 1.414213561.414213561.41421356 → final ≈  4.29  
 
  Result (Pfaffl method with E_target=1.95, E_ref=2.00): Treated is ≈ 4.29-fold upregulated.  
 Note: small differences between ΔΔCt and Pfaffl results arise because real PCR efficiencies deviate from 2.0. Pfaffl is more accurate when efficiencies differ. 
   
  Final interpretation  
 
 Using the simple ΔΔCt method (E = 2):  ~4.59-fold increase  in the target gene in Treated vs Control. 
 Using Pfaffl with given efficiencies (E_target = 1.95, E_ref = 2.00):  ~4.29-fold increase . Both agree: a robust ~4- to 4.6-fold upregulation. 
 
   
  Practical notes & recommendations  
 
 Always run technical triplicates and at least 3 biological replicates for statistical testing. 
 Check amplification efficiencies (standard curves) for both genes; if they differ substantially, use Pfaffl. 
 Inspect melt curves (SYBR) or probe specificity to ensure a single product. 
 Report mean ± SD of ΔCt or the fold-change (often after log₂ transformation) and run appropriate statistics on biological replicates. 
 
 References 
 
 
 Livak, K. J., & Schmittgen, T. D. (2001).  Analysis of relative gene expression data using real-time quantitative PCR and the 2−ΔΔCT method.  Methods, 25(4), 402–408.  https://doi.org/10.1006/meth.2001.1262  
 
 
 Pfaffl, M. W. (2001).  A new mathematical model for relative quantification in real-time RT–PCR.  Nucleic Acids Research, 29(9), e45.  https://doi.org/10.1093/nar/29.9.e45  
 
 
 Schmittgen, T. D., & Livak, K. J. (2008).  Analyzing real-time PCR data by the comparative CT method.  Nature Protocols, 3(6), 1101–1108.  https://doi.org/10.1038/nprot.2008.73  
 
 
 Bustin, S. A., Benes, V., Garson, J. A., et al. (2009).  The MIQE guidelines: Minimum information for publication of quantitative real-time PCR experiments.  Clinical Chemistry, 55(4), 611–622.  https://doi.org/10.1373/clinchem.2008.112797  
 
 
 Applied Biosystems. (2014).  Guide to Performing Relative Quantitation of Gene Expression Using Real-Time Quantitative PCR.  Thermo Fisher Scientific. [Technical Manual]
